title: port podman over to new moby/moby module instead of the old docker/docker one
body:
Image was already moved in https://github.com/containers/container-libs/pull/459

We do vendor update it in https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/27342 which broke swagger so I did a quick fix in https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/27342/commits/6f9bf07a348cb824e0249817812befd54a08ea19 for that to unblock vendoring.

However we need someone to port all the types to use the moby modules in podman so the docker module will get dropped.

---

On another note to we actually benifit from reusing the docker/moby types instead of having our own definitions? All the past updates were quite painful as they keep moving things around so we already spend a ton of work on these changes. And in practice it doesn't gain much, like just because they add new field it still doesn't mean it works in podman becuase podman of course still has to actually populate these fields with our data to return them in the API.
And right now we still do not properly track and implement the new docker API changes so these updates are not helping for that either.

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I can take this up if no one has started the work around this.
I think @jankaluza just started to work on it
My bad, saw that after I had posted the comment.

To give some update. I have the podman locally without any use of docker/docker - it uses just moby/moby. What I need to do now is to go through all the previously deprecated fields with are now removed in the moby/moby and ensure we still return them for older versions of clients.

Hey Podman team, can you clarify your general position regarding the compatibility API please?

In general do you try to keep up with docker changes asap, or did you decide to stick to a specific API version with its schemes, or something else?
I'm asking because there was https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/c9e20280ed2de26ed442a5a335e49a5dafede024 recently that only bumped the version reported by the compat API, but that didn't change a single response of it (removing fields, etc), so I wonder how much we can "trust" the compat API. (hear, if the compat API payloads/responses fully match the version it claims to support)

After @jankaluza 's work, is the claim to fully and exactly support the 1.44-1.52 payload/responses formats? (= match docker 29)
Is the compat API capped to 1.41 / 1.44 formats at the moment? (1.44 is not released, AFAIK)
Or is the compat API a best effort thing and will be/always has been a mixed bag of all formats?

It's not a blame or anything, and I'm fine with any stance you take as long as it is clear, so we (consumers of the API) can know how much we can trust the compat API 🙂

For a long while, I think our stance was "don't touch it aside from bugfixes", but with the Docker API starting to move much faster of late, we are now having to go ahead and start implementing the new versions. We had done a lot of enablement work for the 1.41-1.44 versions of the API already before bumping the version reported (thanks to @inknos for those updates) so it's not as if we bumped to 1.44 without doing anything, but we did not get to all API changes 1.41 to 1.44 before we bumped the min-version so we're still not fully compatible. I don't really like this position - the goal is to be fully compatible with the Docker API in a parameter-for-parameter, response-for-response way - but the majority of the team is currently working on other things and, with Docker bumping their minimum version, it seemed the lesser of two evils.

Right now we're mostly working on getting Podman 6 out the door (second week of May on the timeframe for that) which does not involve further Docker API work. Once that is done, I'm hoping we can get back to working on the API. I would hope that by end of year we are v1.52 compatible (in the fully and exactly support the payload and responses sense).

>  _so it's not as if we bumped to 1.44 without doing anything_

Don't get me wrong I don't underestimate the work of the team here. I simply didn't take a deeper look at the commit history and this bump commit raised this interrogation of "What is exactly supported / What is the exact state of the compat API?" 

The changes since the Docker 29 release took us off guard as well at Portainer and we are only trying to decide what's the best/most stable way to keep Podman into our product and provide the best possible experience (currently limited to the also-docker features) to our users that chose Podman over Docker.

Technically our Podman integration currently relies on our Docker layer & clients leveraging Podman's compat API, but we are in a kind-of deadlock with Podman right now as we have to bump our Docker version to 29, which won't be able to query the compat API as, AFAIK, the 1.44 bump has not been released yet.

We were wondering how trustworthy was the 1.44 version and if it's deemed stable enough for an LTS release. Maybe it's time for us to integrate fully with the podman lib sooner than expected 😊 

Thank you very much for this detailed answer @mheon . We went (and still are going) through the docker 29 update/changes, so we know exactly the pain it is...

1.44 was chosen since it was a) least effort and b) supported by docker "lts" (v25), so it was supported until 2027

See: https://github.com/moby/moby/blob/master/project/BRANCHES-AND-TAGS.md#docker-engine-branches

* https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/27915#issuecomment-3769244409

The newer releases (1.45-1.52) might actually be supported for a shorter time, but there is no more decade support...

Previously it was Docker that had the stable API (v1.24), but now Podman is backward-compatible all the way to 4.0

* https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/27890#issuecomment-3763606824
